failure_surface: Hardcoded payout grid values instead of reading them dynamically from the spreadsheet; variance bucket boundaries may not match the instruction specifications
root_cause: The agent extracted and hard-coded the payout grid values (0.03, 0.04, etc.) directly into Python lists instead of programmatically reading them from the relevant worksheet area. This makes the solution fragile to any variation in the source data structure or values. Additionally, the variance bucket thresholds (≤0%, 0-3%, 3-5%, >5%) were assumed rather than verified against the actual spreadsheet layout.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Replace hardcoded grid values with dynamic reads from the source spreadsheet location. Verify all threshold boundaries by inspecting the relevant cells before implementing conditional logic.
evidence: Agent stated 'Right table: Rows 2-5, Columns P-T (indices 15-19)' but proceeded to hardcode rather than read these values programmatically
Variance bucket functions used fixed thresholds (0.03, 0.05) without verifying against the 'Varince to Goal' section headers in the spreadsheet
Spreadsheet has 23 total rows including header, employee data extends to row 22, but answer_position specified G2:G19 which may exclude some employee records

memory_items.title: Dynamic Lookup Table Reading
memory_items.lesson: Never hard-code lookup table values extracted from spreadsheets; always read them programmatically from their source location to maintain correctness across data variations.
memory_items.generalization: When a spreadsheet contains reference tables (payout grids, tier definitions, rate schedules), use library methods to read those cells dynamically rather than copying values into code constants. Store them as variables populated from the workbook, not literals.

memory_items.title: Verify Conditional Boundaries Against Source Headers
memory_items.lesson: Boundary conditions in if-elif chains (for categorization/bucketing) must be validated against the actual label cells in the spreadsheet, not inferred from task descriptions alone.
memory_items.generalization: Before implementing threshold logic (goal buckets, variance ranges, metric categories), read the header/label cells that define those boundaries. Use the actual cell values as your comparison points rather than assuming integer or decimal cutoffs.

memory_items.title: Handle Partial Employee Ranges Carefully
memory_items.lesson: When the target cell range (answer_position) doesn't cover all employee rows, ensure not-eligible or out-of-range employees are handled appropriately.
memory_items.generalization: Validate that the specified answer_position range includes all applicable data rows. For rows outside the range, confirm whether they should remain unchanged, be explicitly skipped, or require separate handling based on eligibility criteria.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Dynamic Reference Data Extraction
When a spreadsheet contains lookup tables, payout grids, or tier definition matrices, always read the values programmatically from their source cells rather than hard-coding them. These values may vary between files or update over time. Extract them once at initialization and store as variables populated from the workbook.

### Boundary Condition Verification
Before implementing categorical threshold logic (e.g., bucket assignments, grade boundaries, rate tiers), read the actual header/label cells that define those boundaries. Do not assume cutoff values from task descriptions—use the spreadsheet's explicit definitions as your comparison points.

### Target Range Completeness Check
When a task specifies a partial cell range (like G2:G19) but the data extends beyond it, verify whether the incomplete coverage affects calculation accuracy. Determine if additional rows need inclusion or if exclusion is intentional based on eligibility filters.
